title: Algorithms (Python)
description: Build passable interview fluency in Python through practical fundamentals: input parsing, pattern printing, numbers, strings, arithmetic and binary conversion, and arrays.

reviewer.summary: For your Python prep, the goal is to memorize the standard code patterns and library choices that solve beginner interview problems fast. Think in terms of code templates, not vague theory.
interviewFocus: Do you know which Python built-in, method, or module to reach for immediately?
Can you write the standard template from memory with minimal hesitation?
Can you solve simple problems like leap year, reverse string, binary conversion, and array scans in clean Python?
Can you explain why you chose that tool instead of improvising badly?

sections.title: Must Know In Time For Interviews
points: Memorize clean Python templates for input parsing, nested-loop pattern printing, if-else decision problems, string building, arithmetic/base conversion loops, and list traversal.
Know the standard tools that commonly appear in simple coding rounds: input, split, map, list, range, len, collections.Counter, math.gcd, and string slicing.
Practice small real tasks like even or odd, leap year, prime number, palindrome number, factorial, Fibonacci, sum of digits, reverse digits, GCD or LCM, and finding factors or divisors.

sections.title: Suggested Crash-Course Sequence
points: Start with input parsing until reading ints, strings, arrays, and test cases feels automatic.
Then drill pattern printing until row/column reasoning with nested loops feels obvious.
Then drill core number problems like parity, leap year, prime checks, palindrome numbers, factorial, Fibonacci, digit sums, reverse digits, GCD or LCM, and divisors.
Then memorize string templates with slicing, join, and indexed traversal.
After that, rehearse arithmetic and binary conversion loops, then finish with list traversal and sorting helpers.

sections.title: What Usually Fails People
points: Forgetting how to read input quickly and wasting time on boilerplate under pressure.
Forgetting the exact Python built-in or method they actually need.
Writing code from first principles every time instead of using a memorized template.
Making index and condition mistakes on easy problems because the base pattern was not fully internalized.

chapters.title: Input Parsing
chapters.focus: Read ints, strings, lists, and test cases without fumbling Python I/O.

chapters.title: Pattern Printing
chapters.focus: Build row/column thinking with classic star, number, and character patterns.

chapters.title: Numbers
chapters.focus: Master the standard number problems that show up constantly in beginner interviews.

chapters.title: String Manipulation
chapters.focus: Traverse, compare, rebuild, and inspect strings safely.

chapters.title: Arithmetic & Binary Conversion
chapters.focus: Work through number logic, divisibility, and base conversion step by step.

chapters.title: Arrays
chapters.focus: Scan, update, and summarize arrays without index mistakes.

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read one integer `n` and print that same integer.
questions.inputFormat: A single line containing one integer `n`.
questions.outputFormat: Print the integer `n` on its own line.

choices.label: n = int(input())
n = input()
n = list(map(int, input().split()))
n = int(input().split()[0])

questions.explanation: For a single integer, the clean pattern is `n = int(input())`, then print `n`. `input()` alone returns a string, and `.split()` is only needed when the line contains multiple tokens.
questions.studyNote:
Single-int input template

n = int(input())
print(n)

Related basics:
text = input().strip()
nums = list(map(int, input().split()))

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: You are given two integers on the same line. Read them into `a` and `b`, then print their sum.
questions.inputFormat: A single line containing two space-separated integers `a` and `b`.
questions.outputFormat: Print `a + b`.

choices.label:
a, b = map(int, input().split())
print(a + b)

a = int(input())
b = int(input())
print(a + b)

a, b = input().split()
print(a + b)

a = int(input().split()[0])
b = int(input().split()[1])
print(a + b)

questions.explanation: This is the standard Python pattern for space-separated integers on one line: split the line into tokens, convert each token to int, unpack, then use the values.
questions.studyNote:
Read multiple ints from one line

a, b = map(int, input().split())
print(a + b)

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`, then read a list of `n` integers into `arr`, and print the sum of the list.
questions.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer `n`.
The second line contains `n` space-separated integers.
questions.outputFormat: Print the sum of the `n` integers.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
print(sum(arr))

n = int(input())
arr = [input() for _ in range(n)]
print(sum(arr))

n = int(input())
arr = int(input())
print(sum(arr))

arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
n = len(arr)
print(sum(arr))

questions.explanation: A lot of problems use the exact pattern: read the size first, then parse the next line into a list of ints.
questions.studyNote:
Read a list template

n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: The first line gives the number of test cases. Each test case contains one integer `n`. Print `n * 2` for every test case.
questions.inputFormat:
The first line contains one integer `t`, the number of test cases.
Each of the next `t` blocks belongs to one test case.
questions.outputFormat: For each test case, print `n * 2` on its own line.

choices.label:
t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    print(n * 2)

t = int(input())
if t > 0:
    n = int(input())
    print(n * 2)

t = int(input())
while True:
    n = int(input())
    print(n * 2)

t = int(input())
n = int(input())
print(n * 2)

questions.explanation: When a problem gives a test-case count, your Python loop template should already be automatic.
questions.studyNote:
Test-case loop template

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    # read this case
    # solve this case
    # print or collect output

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: The first line contains `t`. Each test case then contains two integers `a` and `b`. Print `a + b` for each test case, one answer per line.
questions.inputFormat:
The first line contains `t`.
Each of the next `t` lines contains two space-separated integers `a` and `b`.
questions.outputFormat: For each test case, print `a + b` on its own line.

choices.label:
t = int(input())
answers = []
for _ in range(t):
    a, b = map(int, input().split())
    answers.append(str(a + b))
print("\n".join(answers))

t = int(input())
answers = []
for _ in range(t):
    a, b = map(int, input().split())
    answers.append(str(a + b))
print("\n".join(answers))

import sys

t = int(input())
out = []
for _ in range(t):
    a, b = map(int, input().split())
    out.append(str(a + b))
sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    a, b = map(int, input().split())
    print("\n".join(str(a + b)))

questions.explanation: This is the standard multi-test-case pattern: read `t`, parse each case, store each answer as a string, then print one answer per line. `sys.stdout.write` is also fine when used the same way.
questions.studyNote:
Output accumulation template

answers = []
answers.append(str(answer))
answers.append(str(other_answer))
print("\n".join(answers))

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read a 2D grid of integers into `matrix`, then print the sum of the first row.
questions.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers `r` and `c`.
The next `r` lines each contain `c` space-separated integers, describing one row of the matrix.
questions.outputFormat: Print the sum of the integers in the first row.

choices.label:
r, c = map(int, input().split())
matrix = [list(map(int, input().split())) for _ in range(r)]
print(sum(matrix[0]))

r, c = map(int, input().split())
matrix = [input().split() for _ in range(c)]
print(sum(matrix[0]))

r = int(input())
c = int(input())
matrix = list(map(int, input().split()))
print(sum(matrix[0]))

matrix = [[0] * c for _ in range(r)]
print(sum(matrix[0]))

questions.explanation: A matrix input pattern is just a repeated row parser: read the dimensions, then read `r` lines, turning each one into a list of ints.
questions.studyNote:
Matrix input template

r, c = map(int, input().split())
matrix = [list(map(int, input().split())) for _ in range(r)]

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print a left triangle of `*` with `n` rows.
questions.outputFormat: Print `n` lines. Line `i` contains exactly `i` stars.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
print("*" * n)

questions.explanation: A left triangle grows one symbol per row, so the row number itself controls how many stars to print.
firstPrinciples: Pattern printing is usually row-by-row construction.
Ask what changes from one row to the next. Here, the star count increases by 1 each row.
tips: For simple patterns, `print("*" * count)` is cleaner than an inner loop.
Map each row number directly to the quantity you need to print.
questions.studyNote:
Left triangle template

n = int(input())
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    print("*" * i)

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print a right triangle of `*` with `n` rows.
questions.outputFormat: Print `n` lines. The last line contains `n` stars, and earlier lines are padded on the left with spaces.
questions.explanation: A right triangle needs two parts per row: leading spaces, then stars.
firstPrinciples: Many patterns are just combinations of blocks: spaces + symbols.
If alignment matters, calculate both the padding and the visible content for each row.
tips: Think in terms of `spaces = n - i` and `stars = i`.
Right-aligned patterns are usually the first place where spaces become part of the output.
questions.studyNote:
Right triangle template

n = int(input())
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    print(" " * (n - i) + "*" * i)

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print a centered pyramid of `*` with `n` rows.
questions.outputFormat: Print `n` lines. Row `i` contains `2*i - 1` stars centered with spaces.
questions.explanation: A centered pyramid grows by odd counts: 1, 3, 5, 7. That is why the star formula is `2*i - 1`.
firstPrinciples: Centered patterns usually have symmetric widths, which often means odd-number sequences.
Figure out two formulas per row: left padding and visible width.
tips: For pyramids, memorize `stars = 2 * i - 1`.
Once you know padding and width, the whole pattern becomes mechanical.
questions.studyNote:
Pyramid template

n = int(input())
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    print(" " * (n - i) + "*" * (2 * i - 1))

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print an inverted centered pyramid of `*` with `n` rows.
questions.outputFormat: Print `n` lines. The first row contains `2*n - 1` stars and each next row shrinks symmetrically.
questions.explanation: An inverted pyramid is the same row formula as a normal pyramid, but the row index shrinks instead of growing.
firstPrinciples: Most inverted patterns are just the original pattern with the loop direction reversed.
If you already know the non-inverted formula, try reusing it with a descending loop first.
tips: Before inventing a new formula, ask whether you can reverse the existing one.
Descending loops like `range(n, 0, -1)` are common in shrinking patterns.
questions.studyNote:
Inverted pyramid template

n = int(input())
for i in range(n, 0, -1):
    print(" " * (n - i) + "*" * (2 * i - 1))

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print a diamond of `*` using a pyramid on top and an inverted pyramid below.
questions.outputFormat: Print `2*n - 1` lines that form a symmetric diamond.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
print("*" * (2 * n - 1))

questions.explanation: A diamond is just two known patterns stitched together. The only subtlety is avoiding printing the middle row twice.
firstPrinciples: Bigger patterns are often compositions of smaller patterns you already know.
When combining top and bottom halves, watch for overlap rows that can accidentally print twice.
tips: Build complex patterns from reusable pieces instead of starting from scratch.
The second loop usually starts at `n - 1`, not `n`, to avoid duplicating the center.

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print Floyd's triangle using numbers starting at 1.
questions.outputFormat: Print `n` rows. Row `i` contains `i` consecutive numbers.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
current = 1
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    row = []
    for _ in range(i):
        row.append(str(current))
        current += 1
    print(" ".join(row))

n = int(input())
current = 1
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    print(current)
    current += i

questions.explanation: Floyd's triangle needs a running counter that continues across rows, so you must keep state outside the inner loop.
firstPrinciples: Some patterns are not purely about row and column positions; they also need persistent state across rows.
If values continue from one row to the next, your counter must live outside the row loop.
tips: Use a `current` variable when the printed value does not reset each row.
Pattern printing is often either formula-per-cell or stateful generation. Decide which one you have early.
questions.studyNote:
Floyd's triangle template

n = int(input())
current = 1
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    row = []
    for _ in range(i):
        row.append(str(current))
        current += 1
    print(" ".join(row))

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print a number triangle where row `i` contains the number `i`, repeated `i` times.
questions.outputFormat: Print `n` rows.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
print("1")

questions.explanation: This pattern resets each row and uses the row number as the printed value.
firstPrinciples: Many number/character triangles are row-driven patterns: the row number determines both count and value.
If every item in a row is the same, build the whole row from that row's identity.
tips: When a row repeats one value, `join` over a generator is often the cleanest output pattern.
Separate `what value to print` from `how many times to print it`.
questions.studyNote:
Number triangle template

n = int(input())
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    print(" ".join(str(i) for _ in range(i)))

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print a character triangle starting from `A`, where row `i` contains the same character repeated `i` times.
questions.outputFormat: Print `n` rows: `A`, `B B`, `C C C`, and so on.
questions.exampleOutput:
A
B B
C C C

choices.label:
n = int(input())
for i in range(n):
    ch = chr(ord('A') + i)
    print(" ".join(ch for _ in range(i + 1)))

n = int(input())
for i in range(n):
    ch = chr(ord('A') + i)
    print(ch * i)

questions.explanation: Character patterns often combine row-based counting with ASCII/Unicode stepping using `ord` and `chr`.
firstPrinciples: Character patterns are usually number patterns plus a mapping from row index to character.
In Python, `ord` and `chr` let you move from letters to numeric positions and back.
tips: Think `row index -> letter`, then print that letter the needed number of times.
If spacing matters, use `' '.join(...)` instead of raw string multiplication.
questions.studyNote:
Character triangle template

n = int(input())
for i in range(n):
    ch = chr(ord('A') + i)
    print(" ".join(ch for _ in range(i + 1)))

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print a hollow square of `*`, where only the border contains stars.
questions.outputFormat: Print `n` rows and `n` columns.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
print("*" * n)

questions.explanation: Hollow patterns depend on border conditions: top row, bottom row, left edge, and right edge.
firstPrinciples: Hollow shapes are border-detection problems.
Ask whether the current cell is on the boundary or inside the shape. Boundaries get symbols; the interior gets spaces.
tips: For square/rectangle borders, first handle the full top and bottom rows, then the middle rows.
If you understand the hollow square, hollow pyramids are the same idea with different boundary math.

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print `Even` if `n` is even, otherwise print `Odd`.
questions.outputFormat: Print `Even` or `Odd`.

choices.label:
n = input()
print("Even")

questions.explanation: Parity is the most basic number check: use `n % 2 == 0` for even, otherwise odd.
firstPrinciples: Even/odd problems are remainder problems. `% 2` tells you whether a number leaves a remainder when split into pairs.
A lot of beginner number questions reduce to classification: compute a small property, then branch with `if/else`.
tips: Memorize `n % 2 == 0` as the default even check.
If the problem only asks for a label like `Even` or `Odd`, do not overcomplicate it with loops or extra variables.

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read a year and print `Leap Year` if it is a leap year, otherwise print `Not Leap Year`.
questions.outputFormat: Print `Leap Year` or `Not Leap Year`.

choices.label:
year = int(input())
print("Leap Year")

questions.explanation: The leap-year rule is ordered: divisible by 400 means leap year, divisible by 100 means not leap year, otherwise divisible by 4 means leap year.
firstPrinciples: Some rule-based problems are about precedence, not just conditions. The order of checks matters because later rules can be overridden by earlier special cases.
When a statement has exceptions to exceptions, write the conditions in the same order the English rule describes them.
tips: Think of leap year as a decision tree: 400 first, then 100, then 4.
If a problem statement has words like `except` or `unless`, expect ordered `if / elif / else` logic.

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print `Prime` if `n` is prime, otherwise print `Not Prime`.
questions.outputFormat: Print `Prime` or `Not Prime`.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
is_prime = True
for i in range(2, int(n ** 0.5) + 1):
    if n % i == 0:
        is_prime = False
        break
print("Prime" if is_prime else "Not Prime")

questions.explanation: A correct prime check handles `n < 2` and tests divisors only up to `sqrt(n)`. Common bugs are using `range(2, int(sqrt(n)))` and missing perfect-square divisors, or forgetting that `0` and `1` are not prime.
firstPrinciples: A number is prime if it has no divisor other than 1 and itself.
Divisors come in pairs. If `i` divides `n`, then `n / i` also divides `n`, so you only need to search up to `sqrt(n)`.
Edge cases matter: `0` and `1` are not prime because prime numbers must have exactly two positive divisors.
tips: Memorize the pattern `for i in range(2, int(n ** 0.5) + 1)`.
When testing divisor-based properties, ask yourself whether you can stop early instead of scanning all the way to `n`.
Perfect squares are the classic off-by-one trap, which is why the `+ 1` matters.

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print `Palindrome` if its digits read the same forward and backward, otherwise print `Not Palindrome`.
questions.outputFormat: Print `Palindrome` or `Not Palindrome`.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
original = n
rev = 0
while n > 0:
    rev = rev * 10 + n % 10
    n //= 10
print("Palindrome" if original == rev else "Not Palindrome")

n = int(input())
print("Palindrome" if n == str(n)[::-1] else "Not Palindrome")

n = int(input())
rev = 0
while n > 0:
    rev += n % 10
    n //= 10
print("Palindrome" if rev == n else "Not Palindrome")

n = int(input())
print("Palindrome")

questions.explanation: For number-palindrome problems, the standard integer approach is to reverse the digits and compare to the original value.
firstPrinciples: Palindrome checks are symmetry checks: the value should look the same from left to right and right to left.
For integer-digit problems, `% 10` extracts the last digit and `//= 10` removes it. That is the core move behind many number manipulations.
tips: If the problem is explicitly about a number, practicing the numeric approach is better than jumping straight to string conversion.
Reversing and comparing to the original works for both palindrome numbers and reverse-digit problems.
questions.studyNote:
Palindrome number template

n = int(input())
original = n
rev = 0
while n > 0:
    rev = rev * 10 + n % 10
    n //= 10
print("Palindrome" if original == rev else "Not Palindrome")

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print `n!`.
questions.outputFormat: Print the factorial of `n`.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
fact = 1
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    fact *= i
print(fact)

n = int(input())
fact = 0
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    fact *= i
print(fact)

n = int(input())
fact = 1
for i in range(n):
    fact += i
print(fact)

n = int(input())
print(n)

questions.explanation: Factorial is repeated multiplication, so start from `1` and multiply by each number from `1` to `n`.
firstPrinciples: Factorial is an accumulation problem. You keep a running answer and update it each step.
Multiplicative accumulations must start from the multiplicative identity, which is `1`, not `0`.
tips: When a problem says `product`, `factorial`, or repeated multiplication, think `ans = 1` and loop.
The additive identity is `0`; the multiplicative identity is `1`. Mixing those up breaks many beginner problems.
questions.studyNote:
Factorial template

n = int(input())
fact = 1
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    fact *= i
print(fact)

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print the `n`th Fibonacci number assuming `F(0) = 0` and `F(1) = 1`.
questions.outputFormat: Print the `n`th Fibonacci number.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
a, b = 0, 1
for _ in range(n):
    a, b = b, a + b
print(a)

n = int(input())
a, b = 0, 1
for _ in range(n):
    a = b
    b = a + b
print(a)

n = int(input())
a, b = 0, 1
for _ in range(n - 1):
    a, b = b, a + b
print(b)

n = int(input())
a, b = 1, 1
for _ in range(n):
    a, b = b, a + b
print(a)

questions.explanation: The standard iterative Fibonacci template updates two running values and prints `a` after `n` transitions. Common mistakes are overwriting `a` before computing the new `b`, getting the loop count off by one, or starting from the wrong base values.
firstPrinciples: Fibonacci is a state-transition problem. At each step, the next state depends on the previous two values.
When two variables both depend on the old state, update them together so you do not lose information.
tips: Tuple assignment like `a, b = b, a + b` is the cleanest Python pattern for simultaneous updates.
For recurrence problems, ask: what state do I need to carry from one iteration to the next?
Always anchor your loop logic against small cases like `n = 0`, `1`, and `2`.
questions.studyNote:
Fibonacci template

n = int(input())
a, b = 0, 1
for _ in range(n):
    a, b = b, a + b
print(a)

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print the sum of its digits.
questions.outputFormat: Print the sum of the digits of `n`.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
total = 0
while n > 0:
    total += n % 10
    n //= 10
print(total)

n = int(input())
total = 1
while n > 0:
    total *= n % 10
    n //= 10
print(total)

n = int(input())
print(n % 10)

n = int(input())
print(n)

questions.explanation: Digit-sum problems use `% 10` to grab the last digit and `//= 10` to remove it.
firstPrinciples: This is a digit-decomposition problem: repeatedly peel off the last digit until the number is exhausted.
Many number problems are just loops that consume digits one at a time.
tips: Use `% 10` when you need the current last digit and `//= 10` when you want to move to the next one.
When the question asks for a sum, initialize with `0`; when it asks for a product, initialize with `1`.
questions.studyNote:
Sum of digits template

n = int(input())
total = 0
while n > 0:
    total += n % 10
    n //= 10
print(total)

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print the number formed by reversing its digits.
questions.outputFormat: Print the reversed-digit number.

choices.label:
n = int(input())
rev = 0
while n > 0:
    rev = rev * 10 + n % 10
    n //= 10
print(rev)

n = int(input())
rev = 0
while n > 0:
    rev += n % 10
    n //= 10
print(rev)

n = int(input())
print(n % 10)

n = int(input())
print(n)

questions.explanation: To reverse digits numerically, multiply the current answer by 10, then add the last digit each step.
firstPrinciples: Reversing digits is a place-value problem. Each old digit becomes a new digit in a shifted position.
Building a number from left to right usually means `ans = ans * 10 + new_digit`.
tips: The pattern `rev = rev * 10 + n % 10` shows up constantly in digit-manipulation problems.
If you understand reverse digits, you already understand half of palindrome-number logic.
questions.studyNote:
Reverse digits template

n = int(input())
rev = 0
while n > 0:
    rev = rev * 10 + n % 10
    n //= 10
print(rev)

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read two integers `a` and `b`. Print their GCD and LCM separated by a space.
questions.inputFormat: A single line containing two space-separated integers `a` and `b`.
questions.outputFormat: Print `gcd lcm`.

choices.label:
a, b = map(int, input().split())
x, y = a, b
while y != 0:
    x, y = y, x % y
gcd = x
lcm = a * b // gcd
print(gcd, lcm)

a, b = map(int, input().split())
gcd = a + b
lcm = a * b
print(gcd, lcm)

a, b = map(int, input().split())
gcd = min(a, b)
lcm = max(a, b)
print(gcd, lcm)

a, b = map(int, input().split())
print(a, b)

questions.explanation: Use Euclid's algorithm for GCD, then compute LCM with `a * b // gcd`.
firstPrinciples: GCD is about shared divisibility; LCM is about the smallest shared multiple.
Euclid's algorithm works because `gcd(a, b)` is the same as `gcd(b, a % b)` until the remainder becomes zero.
Once you know the GCD, the identity `gcd * lcm = a * b` gives you the LCM.
tips: Memorize the Euclid loop exactly once and reuse it everywhere.
If a problem asks for both GCD and LCM, compute GCD first; do not try to brute-force LCM.
questions.studyNote:
GCD / LCM template

a, b = map(int, input().split())
x, y = a, b
while y != 0:
    x, y = y, x % y
gcd = x
lcm = a * b // gcd
print(gcd, lcm)

questions.prompt: Which Python code solves this problem correctly?
questions.problemDescription: Read an integer `n`. Print all of its positive divisors in increasing order, separated by spaces.
questions.outputFormat: Print all positive divisors of `n` in increasing order.
questions.explanation: The simplest divisor template scans from `1` to `n` and keeps numbers that divide `n` evenly. Real mistakes here are skipping `n` with `range(1, n)`, flipping the modulus to `i % n`, or accidentally omitting `1`.
firstPrinciples: A divisor is a number that leaves remainder `0` when dividing `n`.
This is a filtering problem: scan candidates, test a condition, keep the ones that pass.
Many number questions are really candidate-generation plus a divisibility test.
tips: The safe beginner pattern is `for i in range(1, n + 1): if n % i == 0:`.
When you forget whether it is `n % i` or `i % n`, read the condition in English: `i divides n` means `n` divided by `i` leaves remainder `0`.
Once this basic version is comfortable, you can later optimize divisors using paired factors up to `sqrt(n)`.
questions.studyNote:
Factors / divisors template

n = int(input())
ans = []
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    if n % i == 0:
        ans.append(str(i))
print(" ".join(ans))

questions.prompt: Problem: return the reversed version of a string `s`. Which implementation is the strongest simple answer?

choices.label: return s[::-1]
return reverse(s)
return s.reverse()
return ''.join(sorted(s))

questions.explanation: For a direct reverse-string problem, `s[::-1]` is the cleanest Python answer to memorize. It is concise, correct, and widely accepted in interviews unless they explicitly forbid slicing.
questions.studyNote:
Canonical Python palindrome-string template

def is_palindrome(s):
    left = 0
    right = len(s) - 1

    while left < right:
        if s[left] != s[right]:
            return False
        left += 1
        right -= 1

    return True

Also rehearse:
- counting vowels
- reversing a string with slicing or ''.join(reversed(s))
- checking character frequency with Counter

questions.prompt: Problem: convert a non-negative integer `n` to binary yourself, without using `bin(n)`. Which implementation pattern is correct?
questions.codeSnippet:
def to_binary(n):
    # build the binary representation yourself

choices.label:
def to_binary(n):
    return str(n / 2)

questions.explanation: For manual binary conversion, the standard interview-safe loop is divide by 2, record remainders, then reverse the collected digits.

questions.prompt: Problem: return the maximum value in a non-empty list `nums` without using `max(nums)`. Which implementation is correct?

choices.label: return nums.sort()[-1]

questions.explanation: This is the standard running-maximum scan. Starting with `best = nums[0]` avoids bugs on all-negative lists, and updating in one pass is the core pattern to memorize.
questions.studyNote:
Canonical Python list scan template

def max_value(nums):
    best = nums[0]

    for num in nums[1:]:
        if num > best:
            best = num

    return best

Library habits to memorize:
- sorted(nums)
- nums.sort()
- len(nums)
- for num in nums
